title: Error Handling: Surviving One Bad Reading
learningObjectives: Wrap risky operations in try/except instead of letting the program crash on bad input
Catch specific exception types (KeyError, ValueError) rather than a bare except
Use else/finally appropriately when cleanup or fallback behavior is needed
Decide when to handle an error locally versus let it propagate
whyItMatters: Real sensor feeds are messy — a reading might be missing a field, arrive as a string instead of a number, or fail to parse entirely. The Digital Twin cannot crash every time one bad reading arrives out of thousands; it needs to handle the bad one and keep processing the rest.
capstoneContribution: The Digital Twin's fault-tolerant ingestion loop — one malformed reading logs a warning and gets skipped, instead of taking down the entire pipeline.

problem.summary: One malformed reading currently crashes the whole batch.
problem.content: Somewhere in your rolling window of readings, one record has `demand_kw` stored as the string `"4821"` instead of the number `4821`, and another is missing the `demand_kw` key entirely. Right now, either one crashes your whole classification loop.

motivation.summary: One bad reading out of thousands shouldn't take down the pipeline.
motivation.content: In a real deployment, a single malformed reading out of thousands shouldn't take down the whole pipeline. This is the difference between a script that only works on a clean demo dataset and a system that survives real, messy data.

theory.summary: try/except intercepts a matching exception instead of crashing; catch specific types.
theory.content: `try: risky_code() except SomeError as e: handle(e)` runs `risky_code()`, and if it raises an exception matching `SomeError`, control jumps to the except block instead of crashing the program. Catching specific exception types (`KeyError`, `ValueError`) rather than a bare `except:` means you only swallow errors you actually anticipated, and let genuinely unexpected bugs surface loudly. `else` runs only if no exception occurred; `finally` always runs, exception or not — useful for cleanup.

visualExplanation.summary: Malformed readings divert down a logged-and-skipped path; good readings flow straight through.
visualExplanation.content: A reading flows into a try block; a malformed reading diverts down an except path (logged, skipped) while a good reading flows straight through to the classification step — and the loop continues in both cases.

interactiveDemo.summary: Feed a mixed stream of readings through a visual try/except pipeline.
interactiveDemo.content: Feed a stream of readings — some well-formed, some malformed — through a visual try/except pipeline and watch which ones divert to the 'skipped, logged' path versus flow straight through, with a running count of skipped records by reason.

guidedCoding.summary: Follow along: classify readings while catching the two most likely failure modes.

independentCoding.summary: Classify battery readings while counting skips by reason, unaided.
independentCoding.content: Given a list of battery readings where some are missing `soc_percent` and some have it as a non-numeric string, write a loop that classifies the good ones and counts, without crashing, how many were skipped for each reason.

debugging.summary: Narrow a bare except: that's silently hiding a real bug.
debugging.content:
```python
try:
    value = float(r["demmand_kw"])  # typo
except:
    value = 0
```
The bare `except:` is silently masking a typo'd key name that should be fixed, not caught. Narrow the except clause to the specific expected exception type and let the real bug surface.

reflection.summary: A bare except: can hide more than it protects against.
reflection.content: Why is a bare `except:` sometimes more dangerous than not handling the error at all?

teachBack.summary: Explain narrow vs. broad exception catching to someone who thinks broader is always safer.
teachBack.content: Explain the difference between catching `KeyError` specifically and catching `Exception` broadly, to someone who thinks 'catch everything' is always the safer choice.

miniProject.summary: Make the mini project's ingestion loop fault-tolerant.
miniProject.content: Wrap the mini project's ingestion loop in error handling so a malformed reading is logged and skipped instead of crashing the whole batch.

portfolioEvidence.summary: This is the version of the pipeline that survives real, messy data.
portfolioEvidence.content: This fault-tolerant loop is the version of the ingestion pipeline that actually survives contact with real, messy sensor data.

labs.title: Fault-Tolerant Ingestion
labs.description: Sandboxed exercise processing a batch of readings that includes deliberately malformed records (missing keys, wrong types); the learner must classify all good readings and report skip counts by reason without crashing.

simulations.title: Try/Except Flow Visualizer
simulations.description: Shows a stream of readings flowing through a try/except pipeline, with malformed ones diverting to a logged-and-skipped path.

flashcards.front: Why is catching KeyError specifically better than a bare except: in the ingestion loop?
flashcards.back: A bare except: swallows every kind of error, including genuine bugs you didn't anticipate (like a typo'd key name), hiding them instead of surfacing them. Catching KeyError specifically only handles the exact failure mode you expected.

flashcards.front: When does the finally block run in a try/except/finally statement?
flashcards.back: Always — whether or not an exception occurred, and whether or not it was caught. It's used for cleanup that must happen regardless of the outcome.

rubric: Identifies the bare except: as hiding a real bug rather than just accepting it as valid error handling
Catches specific exception types matching the actual anticipated failure modes (KeyError, ValueError), not a blanket catch-all
Fault-tolerant ingestion loop correctly reports skip counts by reason without crashing on any malformed record
